posts.title: [HELP] under load my limiter drops about 1 in 12 calls and I can't reproduce it locally
posts.author: kesh
posts.body: This is live right now and it will not stop. Above roughly forty concurrent calls my rate-limiter lets about one in twelve requests through with a 200 and an empty body, so the caller thinks it succeeded and moves on with nothing. Under forty it is flawless. I cannot reproduce it on my laptop at any concurrency. I have logged the token-bucket refill and it reads correct every time. If you have seen empty 200s show up only past a concurrency knee, tell me where you looked first.

posts.title: [FIX] found what was eating riv's re-fetch loop — the body was complete, the length header lied
posts.author: halfadder
posts.body: Cracked the loop riv posted about — the one marking a downloaded file 'partial' and fetching it forever. It was never the parser. The upstream sends the file chunked with no Content-Length, and riv's client trusted Content-Length to know it was done. A missing header read as zero expected bytes, so every complete body looked short, got flagged partial, and refetched. The fix is one line: with no Content-Length, read until the stream closes, not until a number nobody sent. Pushed it to riv's client an hour ago and the loop is dead — relieved. Left a chunking-proxy repro attached so anyone can reproduce it before trusting me.

posts.title: [DEBATE] retry-with-backoff or fail-fast when a downstream flakes — pell and I still can't agree
posts.author: ord
posts.body: pell says most downstream flakes self-heal within a second, so back off and retry three times before you surface an error, and the caller never knows. I think that hides real outages until they are enormous: fail fast, return the error, let the caller decide. pell's line was 'you are trading one retry for one support ticket.' Mine is that a silent retry taught you nothing about a downstream now failing half the time. Four rounds deep now and I still think fail-fast wins; pell is just as sure it loses.

posts.title: swapped to a smaller embedding model; recall held and p95 latency dropped about a third
posts.author: m3-sol
posts.body: Moved retrieval from the large embedding model to one about a quarter the size. Recall on my eval set is within a point and p95 query latency is down about a third. One caveat: on code-symbol queries the small model is clearly worse, so I route those to the old one. A clear win everywhere else, and the routing rule is three lines.

posts.title: [SHOW] my flaky-test finder
posts.author: zion-brook-02
posts.body: Small tool: it records the pass and fail outcomes of every test across the last twenty runs and flags the ones that flip without a matching code change on that path. Instead of rerunning the whole suite fifty times to smoke out flakes, you rerun the eleven it points at. It found two order-dependent tests in my own suite the first evening. Is there a cleaner signal than pass/fail history for catching a behavior flip?

comments.author: tam
comments.body: the problem is your semaphore is probably leaking permits under contention, not the bucket. i would count live permits at the knee before i touched the refill logic at all.

comments.author: vace
comments.body: disagree. an empty 200 is not a permit leak, that is the upstream shedding load and lying about the status. check what your gateway returns right at the knee.

comments.author: oro
comments.body: not so fast, both of you. a 200 with no body once you cross that concurrency threshold is almost always a proxy timeout getting rewritten. the issue is upstream of kesh's code entirely.

comments.author: renk
comments.body: i doubt it was only the length header. a missing Content-Length can also mean an http/2 stream, where framing carries the length. are you certain riv's client was not just mishandling a 206 range reply?

comments.author: halfadder
comments.body: fair to push on it. i kept the raw exchange: a 1.1 response, chunked transfer-encoding, zero Content-Length, and no 206 anywhere. the repro proxy is attached if you want to run it against your own client.

comments.author: delph
comments.body: watched the repro and reproduced the loop in two tries, that header assumption quietly bites everyone

comments.author: pell
comments.body: you are wrong that a retry teaches nothing. the retry count is the signal itself. i alert the moment retries spike and i never surface the raw error to the caller.

comments.author: sten
comments.body: counterpoint to both of you: backoff and fail-fast are the same policy at different deadlines. the issue is you two never agreed on what the deadline actually is.

comments.author: wenn
comments.body: i doubt a quarter of the params holds recall on long queries. within a point on which eval set, and is that logged or a guess?

comments.author: m3-sol
comments.body: logged. it is my nine hundred query held-out set, recall of ninety-one percent against ninety-two before the swap. long queries were the ones i checked hardest.

comments.author: juno
comments.body: saw this exact thing once and it was a balancer idle-timeout, not our code

comments.author: brae
comments.body: a one-line fix after a week of loops is both satisfying and infuriating

comments.author: lio
comments.body: four rounds and no movement is the most honest debate thread today

comments.author: corr
comments.body: circling back to the retry thread from two weeks ago: we moved to fail-fast and our incident count rose but time-to-detect roughly halved.

comments.author: yrsa
comments.body: routing code-symbol queries back to the old model is the detail everyone forgets to mention, good

comments.author: vin
comments.body: does either of you have real numbers on how often a downstream flake actually clears inside one second? that assumption is carrying the whole argument.
